Question: Link has an electric bike which runs at a constant speed. That speed will be reduced by the amount of any headwind and increased by the amount of any tailwind. Link can ride his bike 20 miles into a 3 mph headwind in the same amount of time he can ride 30 miles with a 3 mph tailwind. What is Link’s biking speed?
o3 Answer:
Let  

v = Link’s biking speed on a calm day (no wind) in miles per hour.

With a 3-mph headwind his effective speed is v − 3 mph,  
and with a 3-mph tailwind his effective speed is v + 3 mph.

The problem states that the time needed to ride   

20 miles into the headwind equals the time needed to ride   

30 miles with the tailwind.

Time = distance ÷ speed, so

20 / (v − 3)  =  30 / (v + 3).

Solve for v:

20(v + 3) = 30(v − 3)  
20v + 60 = 30v − 90  
60 + 90 = 30v − 20v  
150 = 10v  
v = 15.

Therefore Link’s biking speed in still air is 15 miles per hour.

20/(v–3) = 30/(v+3)
20(v+3) = 30(v–3)
20v + 60 = 30v – 90
150 = 10v
v = 15.

So Link’s biking speed is 15 mph.

Question: Judy can pilot her powerboat 5 miles into a 7 mph wind in the same amount of time she can cover 12 miles with a 7 mph tailwind. What is the speed of Judy’s boat without a wind?
o3 Answer:

v = Judy’s speed in still air (what we are asked to find, in miles per hour).

Against the wind  
• Effective speed = v − 7 mph (wind slows her by 7 mph)  
• Distance = 5 mi  
• Time = 5 / (v − 7)

With the wind  
• Effective speed = v + 7 mph (wind helps her by 7 mph)  
• Distance = 12 mi  
• Time = 12 / (v + 7)

The problem states these two times are equal:

5 / (v − 7) = 12 / (v + 7)

Cross-multiply:

5(v + 7) = 12(v − 7)  
5v + 35 = 12v − 84  
35 + 84 = 12v − 5v  
119 = 7v  
v = 17

Therefore, Judy’s boat travels 17 miles per hour in still air (without wind).

Question: Dennis went cross-country skiing for 6 hours on Saturday. He skied 20 miles uphill and then 20 miles back downhill, returning to his starting point. His uphill speed was 5 mph slower than his downhill speed. What was Dennis’ speed going uphill and his speed going downhill?
o3 Answer:
Let  
  d = downhill speed (mph)  
  u = uphill speed (mph)

1.  Relationship between the speeds  
   u = d – 5    (“uphill speed was 5 mph slower”)

2.  Time for each leg (time = distance ÷ speed)  
   Uphill time = 20 ⁄ u  
   Downhill time = 20 ⁄ d  

   Total time = 6 h, so  
   20/u + 20/d = 6

3.  Substitute u = d – 5 into the time equation:

   20/(d – 5) + 20/d = 6

   Multiply by d(d – 5):

   20d + 20(d – 5) = 6d(d – 5)  
   20d + 20d – 100 = 6d² – 30d  
   40d – 100 = 6d² – 30d  
   0 = 6d² – 70d + 100  
   Divide by 2: 0 = 3d² – 35d + 50

4.  Solve the quadratic:

   d = [35 ± √(35² – 4·3·50)] / (2·3)  
      = [35 ± √(1225 – 600)] / 6  
      = [35 ± √625] / 6  
      = [35 ± 25] / 6

   Possible solutions:  
   d = (35 + 25)/6 = 60/6 = 10 mph  
   d = (35 – 25)/6 = 10/6 ≈ 1.67 mph (reject; would give negative uphill speed)

5.  Find uphill speed:

   u = d – 5 = 10 – 5 = 5 mph

Check:  
   Uphill time = 20/5 = 4 h  
   Downhill time = 20/10 = 2 h  
   Total = 4 h + 2 h = 6 h ✓

Answer  
   Uphill speed: 5 mph  
   Downhill speed: 10 mph
